---
title: Grandma's "Famous" Crème Brûlée à la Française
category: Desserts
servings: "6 people"
credits: "Torture Test Fixture"
illustration: assets/illustrations/recipes/torture-test.svg
---

## INGREDIENTS

### Custard

- 500ml crème fraîche épaisse
- 6 jaunes d'œuf (egg yolks)
- 100g sucre de canne, plus extra for the brûlée top
- 1 gousse de vanille, split and scraped
- A pinch of fleur de sel

### To finish

- 30g sucre roux, for caramelizing

## DIRECTIONS

1. Préchauffez le four à 150°C (300°F).
2. Warm the crème fraîche with the split vanilla gousse until just simmering; remove from heat and infuse for 10 minutes.
3. Whisk the jaunes d'œuf with the sucre until pale, then temper slowly with the warm cream, stirring constantly.
4. Strain through a fine sieve into a jug, then divide among ramekins set in a bain-marie.
5. Bake until the custard is set at the edges but still trembles gently in the centre, about 35–40 minutes.
6. Cool, then chill for at least 4 hours — overnight is best.
7. Just before serving, sprinkle an even layer of sucre roux over each ramekin and caramelize with a torch until deeply amber and crackling.

## NOTES

This recipe is transcribed from a handwritten notebook page with several
crossed-out words and a marginal note in a different hand, so treat the
quantities below as best-effort readings rather than certainties — exactly
the kind of provenance detail that makes archival transcription slower than
copying a printed recipe, and exactly the kind of content this fixture
exists to exercise.

The original quantity for the crème fraîche was written as "500ml" but the
zero was smudged and could plausibly be read as "50Oml" (a stray letter) or
even "5OOml" — context from the rest of the recipe (six egg yolks, six
ramekins) makes 500ml the only quantity that produces a sensible custard, so
that's the reading used here. A second, unrelated hand added "attention à la
cuisson !" (careful with the baking!) in the margin next to step 5, which
reads as a warning about overbaking rather than a change to the method
itself, so it isn't reflected as a separate step.

Several substitution notes accumulated in the notebook's margins over what
looks like years of repeated use: a note from what appears to be a later
year suggests demerara sugar can stand in for the sucre roux topping when
it isn't available, though the crack is said to be slightly less clean. A
different marginal note, in pencil and mostly faded, mentions doubling the
vanilla gousse "for special occasions" — Noël is specifically named — which
is preserved here as a footnote rather than folded into the main method
since it clearly wasn't the everyday version of the recipe.

There is also a longer aside, written across the bottom margin and
continuing onto the back of the page, about a torch malfunctioning one
Christmas and the crème brûlée finishing under the grill (broiler) instead
— apparently successfully, though "il faut surveiller de très, très près"
(you have to watch it very, very closely) is underlined twice. That method
isn't included as a primary instruction here because a torch gives much more
even, controllable results, but it's worth knowing as a fallback: highest
grill setting, ramekins as close to the element as the oven allows, and eyes
on it the entire time, since the sugar can go from perfectly amber to burnt
in a matter of seconds under dry heat.

Finally, the page notes that this recipe "ne pardonne pas" (doesn't
forgive) rushed custard-cooking — pulling the ramekins from the bain-marie
too early, before the edges have properly set, is called out as the single
most common failure, more so than any issue with the caramelized top. Château
crème, gousse, brûlée, café, œuf, à la française — the accented and ligature
characters throughout this note are deliberate, exercising the same UTF-8
handling path that a real transcription of French, Portuguese, or any other
accented-language source content would need.
